.opencode/specs/system-deep-loop/031-deep-loop-gpt-reliability/001-research-and-diagnosis/001-gpt-behavioral-hardening-research/research/lineages/gpt-fast-high/iterations/iteration-026.md:8:- Provider/runtime state can alter benchmark validity, so phase 008 must record OpenCode version, provider list, env guard, and command form.
.opencode/specs/system-deep-loop/z_archive/012-deep-improvement-guarded-refine-hardening/004-fixture-and-grader-infrastructure/spec.md:12:2. **Held-out / gold-set convention (T1, council).** Document and enforce the split: visible optimization fixtures vs held-out promotion gates the proposer never sees, plus an optional human-anchored gold set with locked target scores. Convention lives in deep-improvement `references/shared/`; Adversarial seeds must not be enumerated in repo files the proposer reads (the pilot's T4 fixture published its own answers).
.opencode/specs/system-deep-loop/z_archive/009-deep-loop-parallel-fanout/008-deep-review/review/iterations/iteration-015.md:6956:.opencode/skills/deep-review/changelog/v1.3.1.0.md:19:The skill keeps a small SQLite cache of "coverage graph" nodes and edges - dimensions, files, findings, evidence, and their relationships - so future iterations can reason about what has already been covered. The cache was shared across all review sessions in a spec folder. The primary key on each row was the bare logical id (`d-correctness`, `f-001`, etc.). If two reviewers ran concurrent sessions against the same spec folder and both happened to emit a node with the same logical id, the second write overwrote the first. Filtered reads still worked, but the underlying rows were corrupted. The earlier phase-008 session-isolation test only checked filtered reads over disjoint fixtures, so it never caught the collision path. The cache schema is now on version 2. Node and edge tables use a composite primary key built from spec folder, loop type, session id, and logical id. The database migrates by dropping the old tables and recreating the new ones on first open - the cache is a derived artifact, not durable state, so a drop-and-recreate is safe. The session-isolation test now has a dedicated "shared-ID collisions" block that upserts identical node and edge ids across two sessions and asserts both rows survive with their own content. A future phase that introduces real cross-session analytics on top of this cache will find it correct from day one instead of walking into a silent-overwrite trap.
.opencode/specs/system-deep-loop/z_archive/008-deep-improvement-skill-benchmark-mode/011-sk-code-routing-efficiency-remediation/research/iterations/iteration-001.md:53:- What is the live D3 score if we replay LS-001/SD-001/CS-001 against a surface-nested map? Need the gold sets per scenario to compute the recall-vs-efficiency frontier (REQ-002) — the benchmark stores `wastedCount` but not the explicit gold path list; recover from the scenario fixtures.
.opencode/specs/system-deep-loop/z_archive/008-deep-improvement-skill-benchmark-mode/011-sk-code-routing-efficiency-remediation/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:12:3. Read `score-skill-benchmark.cjs` — the exact D3 (`1 - wasted/routed`) and D2 (`resourceRecall`) math, and which gold set they score against.
.opencode/specs/system-deep-loop/z_archive/008-deep-improvement-skill-benchmark-mode/011-sk-code-routing-efficiency-remediation/research/iterations/iteration-002.md:25:### F11 — FRONTIER QUANTIFIED: the gold sets and the exact recall/efficiency numbers (REQ-002)
.opencode/specs/system-deep-loop/z_archive/008-deep-improvement-skill-benchmark-mode/011-sk-code-routing-efficiency-remediation/research/iterations/iteration-003.md:89:- **No post-fix D3 was executed.** This research is charter-bound to "no router edit." The frontier, gold sets, and waste composition are computed from primary data, but the actual post-fix D3 number requires running `routeSkillResources` against a modified map — that is the FIRST thing the build phase must verify live.
.opencode/specs/system-deep-loop/z_archive/008-deep-improvement-skill-benchmark-mode/011-sk-code-routing-efficiency-remediation/research/research.md:26:## 4. Quantified Frontier (recovered gold sets)
.opencode/specs/system-speckit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/research/008-caura-memclaw-fleet-memory-teachings/iterations/iteration-012.md:10:The core philosophical move is that accuracy is judged over the retrieval-then-answer pipeline: “LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set,” because the pipeline as a whole gets product credit (`docs/performance.md:27`, `docs/performance.md:29`). Token efficiency is normalized against a “no memory system” baseline: tokens sent to the answering LLM divided by prompt plus full prior context (`docs/performance.md:30`). Latency is p50/p95 for `POST /search` under warm-cache, single-tenant load, with cold-cache caveat (`docs/performance.md:31`).
.opencode/specs/system-speckit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/research/008-caura-memclaw-fleet-memory-teachings/iterations/iteration-012.md:21:1. **Claim** · Add an end-task LLM-judge evaluation layer above channel ablation, so memory quality is scored on whether retrieved memory helps answer or complete a developer task, not only on Recall@20 or channel deltas. **Evidence** · MemClaw defines accuracy as “LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set,” and gives credit to the retrieval-then-answer pipeline (`docs/performance.md:29`). **Maps-to** · new sub-packet: `developer-memory-end-task-judge`; integrate with `eval_run_ablation` by running judge scoring after fused retrieval variants. **Verdict** · ADAPT. **Risk** · LLM judges can be noisy and prompt-sensitive; Spec Kit needs stable rubrics and small gold developer tasks. **Confidence** · 0.86. **Why it transfers (or not)** · The benchmark numbers do not transfer because Spec Kit is local/spec-corpus shaped, but pipeline-level judging transfers directly: the user cares whether continuity memory improves implementation decisions.
.opencode/specs/system-speckit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/research/008-caura-memclaw-fleet-memory-teachings/iterations/iteration-012.md:41:3. What small developer-memory gold set best represents Spec Kit: resume task, bug-fix context, architectural decision lookup, or handover continuity?
.opencode/specs/system-speckit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/research/008-caura-memclaw-fleet-memory-teachings/prompts/iteration-020.out:11:{"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1780729336278,"sessionID":"ses_164436585ffexoEvsPd4ItVAfq","part":{"type":"tool","tool":"read","callID":"call_8R2HTr2nOuDJrbn12q70KlRB","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"filePath":"/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md","offset":1,"limit":320},"output":"<path>/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md</path>\n<type>file</type>\n<content>\n1: # Performance & Benchmarks\n2: \n3: Operator-grade companion to the public benchmarks write-up. The blog answers *\"is this credible?\"*; this document answers *\"what should I expect in my system, and what can these numbers not tell me?\"*\n4: \n5: ## Public benchmark results\n6: \n7: |  | LoCoMo | LongMemEval | Search latency |\n8: |---|---|---|---|\n9: | Accuracy (LLM-judge) | **77.6%** | **72.5%** | — |\n10: | Token savings vs full context | **96.6%** | **98.2%** | — |\n11: | Latency | — | — | **23 ms p50 · 27 ms p95** (warm) |\n12: \n13: LoCoMo and LongMemEval are the two most-cited public agent-memory benchmarks. Both measure one agent, one user, one long conversation — the single-chatbot shape. Accuracy across the leading systems (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) clusters in a narrow band.\n14: \n15: **Source:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19).\n16: \n17: **Last updated:** 2026-04-19. These numbers move when we re-run; check the blog for the current canonical version.\n18: \n19: ## What we optimize for\n20: \n21: Accuracy sits inside the leading cluster. That's not the axis we push hardest along.\n22: \n23: - **Latency** — a few hundred ms of search disappears behind one LLM call when you run one agent. The same overhead, multiplied across thousands of agents making millions of recall calls a day, decides whether a deployment is viable.\n24: - **Token efficiency** — recall returns the relevant slice, not the full transcript. Token savings vs sending the full context to the LLM: 96–98% on the two benchmarks. That ratio is the bill at fleet scale.\n25: - **Governance correctness** — write a memory at the wrong scope and you've leaked data across teams. The retrieval surface enforces scope filtering by default; the audit log records every cross-scope read.\n26: \n27: ## What we measure\n28: \n29: - **Accuracy** — LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set. The retrieval-then-answer pipeline as a whole gets the credit; this is the metric that maps to product behavior.\n30: - **Token efficiency** — total tokens sent to the answering LLM, divided by the same prompt + full prior context (the \"no memory system\" baseline).\n31: - **Search latency** — p50 / p95 of `POST /search` against a warm cache, single-tenant load. Cold-cache p50 is higher; we publish warm because that's the steady-state condition under real load.\n32: \n33: ## What these benchmarks can't measure\n34: \n35: Single-agent benchmarks can't ask:\n36: \n37: - Did agent #17's mistake this morning prevent agents #1–#40 from repeating it this afternoon?\n38: - Does a new agent joining the fleet inherit what the fleet already knows, or start from zero?\n39: - Is a memory written by the sales fleet visible — or correctly invisible — to an agent in support?\n40: - Does cross-tenant data ever leak when the recall query is ambiguous?\n41: \n42: These are the questions that decide whether a memory system is deployable inside a company. MemClaw was designed around them — scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet), per-agent trust tiers, PII quarantine before cross-fleet exposure, full audit log, the `memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights` outcome-propagation loop. **None of this moves a recall@k number. All of it moves whether you can deploy.**\n43: \n44: The field needs a benchmark for the fleet-shaped problem. We're working toward one. If you're thinking about this too, the [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/aNfpgfpj) is open.\n45: \n46: ## How MemClaw compares\n47: \n48: For a single chatbot, the public-benchmark leaders (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) cluster in a narrow accuracy band — the choice usually comes down to stack fit, latency, and token budget.\n49: \n50: MemClaw differentiates on the dimensions a single-agent benchmark can't see:\n51: \n52: | Dimension | Why it matters at fleet scale |\n53: |---|---|\n54: | Scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet) | A write at the wrong scope is a data leak across teams |\n55: | Per-agent trust tiers | Lets you trust some agents more than others without rewriting the recall path |\n56: | Cross-agent outcome propagation (`memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights`) | One agent's mistake becomes a preventive rule the rest of the fleet sees before repeating it |\n57: | Latency at fleet load | 23 ms p50 search × millions of calls/day stays affordable; 250 ms doesn't |\n58: | Token efficiency | 96–98% savings vs full context is the bill, not a microbenchmark curiosity |\n59: \n60: ## What to verify in your own deployment\n61: \n62: The published numbers are warm-cache, single-tenant, on our reference hardware. Before relying on them in capacity planning:\n63: \n64: - Run [`/whoami`](integration-without-plugin.md#2-verify-your-identity-whoami) round-trips against your deployment to anchor a baseline.\n65: - Hit `POST /search` under your expected concurrency to confirm latency holds — the search-path optimizer assumes a warm pgvector cache.\n66: - Audit `tenant_id` and `fleet_id` filtering on every recall path you care about; the test suite covers scope correctness, but your tenancy model is yours to validate.\n67: \n68: For benchmark methodology, the raw harness, and the underlying scoring code, see the [`keystone-benchmark`](https://github.com/caura-ai/keystone-benchmark) repo (in development).\n69: \n70: ## Sources\n71: \n72: - **Blog write-up:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19)\n73: - **Public benchmarks:** [LoCoMo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.17753), [LongMemEval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10813)\n\n(End of file - total 73 lines)\n</content>","metadata":{"preview":"# Performance & Benchmarks\n\nOperator-grade companion to the public benchmarks write-up. The blog answers *\"is this credible?\"*; this document answers *\"what should I expect in my system, and what can these numbers not tell me?\"*\n\n## Public benchmark results\n\n|  | LoCoMo | LongMemEval | Search latency |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Accuracy (LLM-judge) | **77.6%** | **72.5%** | — |\n| Token savings vs full context | **96.6%** | **98.2%** | — |\n| Latency | — | — | **23 ms p50 · 27 ms p95** (warm) |\n\nLoCoMo and LongMemEval are the two most-cited public agent-memory benchmarks. Both measure one agent, one user, one long conversation — the single-chatbot shape. Accuracy across the leading systems (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) clusters in a narrow band.\n\n**Source:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19).\n\n**Last updated:** 2026-04-19. These numbers move when we re-run; check the blog for the current canonical version.\n\n## What we optimize for\n","truncated":false,"loaded":[],"display":{"type":"file","path":"/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md","text":"# Performance & Benchmarks\n\nOperator-grade companion to the public benchmarks write-up. The blog answers *\"is this credible?\"*; this document answers *\"what should I expect in my system, and what can these numbers not tell me?\"*\n\n## Public benchmark results\n\n|  | LoCoMo | LongMemEval | Search latency |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Accuracy (LLM-judge) | **77.6%** | **72.5%** | — |\n| Token savings vs full context | **96.6%** | **98.2%** | — |\n| Latency | — | — | **23 ms p50 · 27 ms p95** (warm) |\n\nLoCoMo and LongMemEval are the two most-cited public agent-memory benchmarks. Both measure one agent, one user, one long conversation — the single-chatbot shape. Accuracy across the leading systems (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) clusters in a narrow band.\n\n**Source:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19).\n\n**Last updated:** 2026-04-19. These numbers move when we re-run; check the blog for the current canonical version.\n\n## What we optimize for\n\nAccuracy sits inside the leading cluster. That's not the axis we push hardest along.\n\n- **Latency** — a few hundred ms of search disappears behind one LLM call when you run one agent. The same overhead, multiplied across thousands of agents making millions of recall calls a day, decides whether a deployment is viable.\n- **Token efficiency** — recall returns the relevant slice, not the full transcript. Token savings vs sending the full context to the LLM: 96–98% on the two benchmarks. That ratio is the bill at fleet scale.\n- **Governance correctness** — write a memory at the wrong scope and you've leaked data across teams. The retrieval surface enforces scope filtering by default; the audit log records every cross-scope read.\n\n## What we measure\n\n- **Accuracy** — LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set. The retrieval-then-answer pipeline as a whole gets the credit; this is the metric that maps to product behavior.\n- **Token efficiency** — total tokens sent to the answering LLM, divided by the same prompt + full prior context (the \"no memory system\" baseline).\n- **Search latency** — p50 / p95 of `POST /search` against a warm cache, single-tenant load. Cold-cache p50 is higher; we publish warm because that's the steady-state condition under real load.\n\n## What these benchmarks can't measure\n\nSingle-agent benchmarks can't ask:\n\n- Did agent #17's mistake this morning prevent agents #1–#40 from repeating it this afternoon?\n- Does a new agent joining the fleet inherit what the fleet already knows, or start from zero?\n- Is a memory written by the sales fleet visible — or correctly invisible — to an agent in support?\n- Does cross-tenant data ever leak when the recall query is ambiguous?\n\nThese are the questions that decide whether a memory system is deployable inside a company. MemClaw was designed around them — scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet), per-agent trust tiers, PII quarantine before cross-fleet exposure, full audit log, the `memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights` outcome-propagation loop. **None of this moves a recall@k number. All of it moves whether you can deploy.**\n\nThe field needs a benchmark for the fleet-shaped problem. We're working toward one. If you're thinking about this too, the [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/aNfpgfpj) is open.\n\n## How MemClaw compares\n\nFor a single chatbot, the public-benchmark leaders (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) cluster in a narrow accuracy band — the choice usually comes down to stack fit, latency, and token budget.\n\nMemClaw differentiates on the dimensions a single-agent benchmark can't see:\n\n| Dimension | Why it matters at fleet scale |\n|---|---|\n| Scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet) | A write at the wrong scope is a data leak across teams |\n| Per-agent trust tiers | Lets you trust some agents more than others without rewriting the recall path |\n| Cross-agent outcome propagation (`memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights`) | One agent's mistake becomes a preventive rule the rest of the fleet sees before repeating it |\n| Latency at fleet load | 23 ms p50 search × millions of calls/day stays affordable; 250 ms doesn't |\n| Token efficiency | 96–98% savings vs full context is the bill, not a microbenchmark curiosity |\n\n## What to verify in your own deployment\n\nThe published numbers are warm-cache, single-tenant, on our reference hardware. Before relying on them in capacity planning:\n\n- Run [`/whoami`](integration-without-plugin.md#2-verify-your-identity-whoami) round-trips against your deployment to anchor a baseline.\n- Hit `POST /search` under your expected concurrency to confirm latency holds — the search-path optimizer assumes a warm pgvector cache.\n- Audit `tenant_id` and `fleet_id` filtering on every recall path you care about; the test suite covers scope correctness, but your tenancy model is yours to validate.\n\nFor benchmark methodology, the raw harness, and the underlying scoring code, see the [`keystone-benchmark`](https://github.com/caura-ai/keystone-benchmark) repo (in development).\n\n## Sources\n\n- **Blog write-up:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19)\n- **Public benchmarks:** [LoCoMo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.17753), [LongMemEval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10813)","lineStart":1,"lineEnd":73,"totalLines":73,"truncated":false}},"title":".opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md","time":{"start":1780729336260,"end":1780729336269}},"metadata":{"openai":{"itemId":"fc_0b8394104b5c6f4a016a23c5f82e2081919bbebedfcdb8d603"}},"id":"prt_e9bbd51bc0015i0okwulcnK7J3","sessionID":"ses_164436585ffexoEvsPd4ItVAfq","messageID":"msg_e9bbd1f3f0018sozGKhpasWmZk"}}
.opencode/specs/system-speckit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/research/008-caura-memclaw-fleet-memory-teachings/prompts/iteration-012.out:9:{"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1780728402900,"sessionID":"ses_164517a7effe3vNYDW5PuSLbjT","part":{"type":"tool","tool":"read","callID":"call_0EtwB0JrizQB8dZ7Us869SnT","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"filePath":"/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md","offset":1,"limit":240},"output":"<path>/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md</path>\n<type>file</type>\n<content>\n1: # Performance & Benchmarks\n2: \n3: Operator-grade companion to the public benchmarks write-up. The blog answers *\"is this credible?\"*; this document answers *\"what should I expect in my system, and what can these numbers not tell me?\"*\n4: \n5: ## Public benchmark results\n6: \n7: |  | LoCoMo | LongMemEval | Search latency |\n8: |---|---|---|---|\n9: | Accuracy (LLM-judge) | **77.6%** | **72.5%** | — |\n10: | Token savings vs full context | **96.6%** | **98.2%** | — |\n11: | Latency | — | — | **23 ms p50 · 27 ms p95** (warm) |\n12: \n13: LoCoMo and LongMemEval are the two most-cited public agent-memory benchmarks. Both measure one agent, one user, one long conversation — the single-chatbot shape. Accuracy across the leading systems (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) clusters in a narrow band.\n14: \n15: **Source:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19).\n16: \n17: **Last updated:** 2026-04-19. These numbers move when we re-run; check the blog for the current canonical version.\n18: \n19: ## What we optimize for\n20: \n21: Accuracy sits inside the leading cluster. That's not the axis we push hardest along.\n22: \n23: - **Latency** — a few hundred ms of search disappears behind one LLM call when you run one agent. The same overhead, multiplied across thousands of agents making millions of recall calls a day, decides whether a deployment is viable.\n24: - **Token efficiency** — recall returns the relevant slice, not the full transcript. Token savings vs sending the full context to the LLM: 96–98% on the two benchmarks. That ratio is the bill at fleet scale.\n25: - **Governance correctness** — write a memory at the wrong scope and you've leaked data across teams. The retrieval surface enforces scope filtering by default; the audit log records every cross-scope read.\n26: \n27: ## What we measure\n28: \n29: - **Accuracy** — LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set. The retrieval-then-answer pipeline as a whole gets the credit; this is the metric that maps to product behavior.\n30: - **Token efficiency** — total tokens sent to the answering LLM, divided by the same prompt + full prior context (the \"no memory system\" baseline).\n31: - **Search latency** — p50 / p95 of `POST /search` against a warm cache, single-tenant load. Cold-cache p50 is higher; we publish warm because that's the steady-state condition under real load.\n32: \n33: ## What these benchmarks can't measure\n34: \n35: Single-agent benchmarks can't ask:\n36: \n37: - Did agent #17's mistake this morning prevent agents #1–#40 from repeating it this afternoon?\n38: - Does a new agent joining the fleet inherit what the fleet already knows, or start from zero?\n39: - Is a memory written by the sales fleet visible — or correctly invisible — to an agent in support?\n40: - Does cross-tenant data ever leak when the recall query is ambiguous?\n41: \n42: These are the questions that decide whether a memory system is deployable inside a company. MemClaw was designed around them — scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet), per-agent trust tiers, PII quarantine before cross-fleet exposure, full audit log, the `memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights` outcome-propagation loop. **None of this moves a recall@k number. All of it moves whether you can deploy.**\n43: \n44: The field needs a benchmark for the fleet-shaped problem. We're working toward one. If you're thinking about this too, the [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/aNfpgfpj) is open.\n45: \n46: ## How MemClaw compares\n47: \n48: For a single chatbot, the public-benchmark leaders (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) cluster in a narrow accuracy band — the choice usually comes down to stack fit, latency, and token budget.\n49: \n50: MemClaw differentiates on the dimensions a single-agent benchmark can't see:\n51: \n52: | Dimension | Why it matters at fleet scale |\n53: |---|---|\n54: | Scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet) | A write at the wrong scope is a data leak across teams |\n55: | Per-agent trust tiers | Lets you trust some agents more than others without rewriting the recall path |\n56: | Cross-agent outcome propagation (`memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights`) | One agent's mistake becomes a preventive rule the rest of the fleet sees before repeating it |\n57: | Latency at fleet load | 23 ms p50 search × millions of calls/day stays affordable; 250 ms doesn't |\n58: | Token efficiency | 96–98% savings vs full context is the bill, not a microbenchmark curiosity |\n59: \n60: ## What to verify in your own deployment\n61: \n62: The published numbers are warm-cache, single-tenant, on our reference hardware. Before relying on them in capacity planning:\n63: \n64: - Run [`/whoami`](integration-without-plugin.md#2-verify-your-identity-whoami) round-trips against your deployment to anchor a baseline.\n65: - Hit `POST /search` under your expected concurrency to confirm latency holds — the search-path optimizer assumes a warm pgvector cache.\n66: - Audit `tenant_id` and `fleet_id` filtering on every recall path you care about; the test suite covers scope correctness, but your tenancy model is yours to validate.\n67: \n68: For benchmark methodology, the raw harness, and the underlying scoring code, see the [`keystone-benchmark`](https://github.com/caura-ai/keystone-benchmark) repo (in development).\n69: \n70: ## Sources\n71: \n72: - **Blog write-up:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19)\n73: - **Public benchmarks:** [LoCoMo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.17753), [LongMemEval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10813)\n\n(End of file - total 73 lines)\n</content>","metadata":{"preview":"# Performance & Benchmarks\n\nOperator-grade companion to the public benchmarks write-up. The blog answers *\"is this credible?\"*; this document answers *\"what should I expect in my system, and what can these numbers not tell me?\"*\n\n## Public benchmark results\n\n|  | LoCoMo | LongMemEval | Search latency |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Accuracy (LLM-judge) | **77.6%** | **72.5%** | — |\n| Token savings vs full context | **96.6%** | **98.2%** | — |\n| Latency | — | — | **23 ms p50 · 27 ms p95** (warm) |\n\nLoCoMo and LongMemEval are the two most-cited public agent-memory benchmarks. Both measure one agent, one user, one long conversation — the single-chatbot shape. Accuracy across the leading systems (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) clusters in a narrow band.\n\n**Source:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19).\n\n**Last updated:** 2026-04-19. These numbers move when we re-run; check the blog for the current canonical version.\n\n## What we optimize for\n","truncated":false,"loaded":[],"display":{"type":"file","path":"/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md","text":"# Performance & Benchmarks\n\nOperator-grade companion to the public benchmarks write-up. The blog answers *\"is this credible?\"*; this document answers *\"what should I expect in my system, and what can these numbers not tell me?\"*\n\n## Public benchmark results\n\n|  | LoCoMo | LongMemEval | Search latency |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Accuracy (LLM-judge) | **77.6%** | **72.5%** | — |\n| Token savings vs full context | **96.6%** | **98.2%** | — |\n| Latency | — | — | **23 ms p50 · 27 ms p95** (warm) |\n\nLoCoMo and LongMemEval are the two most-cited public agent-memory benchmarks. Both measure one agent, one user, one long conversation — the single-chatbot shape. Accuracy across the leading systems (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) clusters in a narrow band.\n\n**Source:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19).\n\n**Last updated:** 2026-04-19. These numbers move when we re-run; check the blog for the current canonical version.\n\n## What we optimize for\n\nAccuracy sits inside the leading cluster. That's not the axis we push hardest along.\n\n- **Latency** — a few hundred ms of search disappears behind one LLM call when you run one agent. The same overhead, multiplied across thousands of agents making millions of recall calls a day, decides whether a deployment is viable.\n- **Token efficiency** — recall returns the relevant slice, not the full transcript. Token savings vs sending the full context to the LLM: 96–98% on the two benchmarks. That ratio is the bill at fleet scale.\n- **Governance correctness** — write a memory at the wrong scope and you've leaked data across teams. The retrieval surface enforces scope filtering by default; the audit log records every cross-scope read.\n\n## What we measure\n\n- **Accuracy** — LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set. The retrieval-then-answer pipeline as a whole gets the credit; this is the metric that maps to product behavior.\n- **Token efficiency** — total tokens sent to the answering LLM, divided by the same prompt + full prior context (the \"no memory system\" baseline).\n- **Search latency** — p50 / p95 of `POST /search` against a warm cache, single-tenant load. Cold-cache p50 is higher; we publish warm because that's the steady-state condition under real load.\n\n## What these benchmarks can't measure\n\nSingle-agent benchmarks can't ask:\n\n- Did agent #17's mistake this morning prevent agents #1–#40 from repeating it this afternoon?\n- Does a new agent joining the fleet inherit what the fleet already knows, or start from zero?\n- Is a memory written by the sales fleet visible — or correctly invisible — to an agent in support?\n- Does cross-tenant data ever leak when the recall query is ambiguous?\n\nThese are the questions that decide whether a memory system is deployable inside a company. MemClaw was designed around them — scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet), per-agent trust tiers, PII quarantine before cross-fleet exposure, full audit log, the `memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights` outcome-propagation loop. **None of this moves a recall@k number. All of it moves whether you can deploy.**\n\nThe field needs a benchmark for the fleet-shaped problem. We're working toward one. If you're thinking about this too, the [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/aNfpgfpj) is open.\n\n## How MemClaw compares\n\nFor a single chatbot, the public-benchmark leaders (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) cluster in a narrow accuracy band — the choice usually comes down to stack fit, latency, and token budget.\n\nMemClaw differentiates on the dimensions a single-agent benchmark can't see:\n\n| Dimension | Why it matters at fleet scale |\n|---|---|\n| Scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet) | A write at the wrong scope is a data leak across teams |\n| Per-agent trust tiers | Lets you trust some agents more than others without rewriting the recall path |\n| Cross-agent outcome propagation (`memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights`) | One agent's mistake becomes a preventive rule the rest of the fleet sees before repeating it |\n| Latency at fleet load | 23 ms p50 search × millions of calls/day stays affordable; 250 ms doesn't |\n| Token efficiency | 96–98% savings vs full context is the bill, not a microbenchmark curiosity |\n\n## What to verify in your own deployment\n\nThe published numbers are warm-cache, single-tenant, on our reference hardware. Before relying on them in capacity planning:\n\n- Run [`/whoami`](integration-without-plugin.md#2-verify-your-identity-whoami) round-trips against your deployment to anchor a baseline.\n- Hit `POST /search` under your expected concurrency to confirm latency holds — the search-path optimizer assumes a warm pgvector cache.\n- Audit `tenant_id` and `fleet_id` filtering on every recall path you care about; the test suite covers scope correctness, but your tenancy model is yours to validate.\n\nFor benchmark methodology, the raw harness, and the underlying scoring code, see the [`keystone-benchmark`](https://github.com/caura-ai/keystone-benchmark) repo (in development).\n\n## Sources\n\n- **Blog write-up:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19)\n- **Public benchmarks:** [LoCoMo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.17753), [LongMemEval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10813)","lineStart":1,"lineEnd":73,"totalLines":73,"truncated":false}},"title":".opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md","time":{"start":1780728402872,"end":1780728402899}},"metadata":{"openai":{"itemId":"fc_099df243cd85a8af016a23c252c5e08191b51aa75634cbb12c"}},"id":"prt_e9baf138a001dA0rY8BQwa0FZz","sessionID":"ses_164517a7effe3vNYDW5PuSLbjT","messageID":"msg_e9baef3c000158kSGH752RKWiH"}}
.opencode/specs/system-speckit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/research/008-caura-memclaw-fleet-memory-teachings/prompts/iteration-012.out:73:{"type":"text","timestamp":1780728573686,"sessionID":"ses_164517a7effe3vNYDW5PuSLbjT","part":{"id":"prt_e9bb0df29001nS1obRNVeYqsf0","messageID":"msg_e9bb0b10c001MtkTHOXHrQTBQw","sessionID":"ses_164517a7effe3vNYDW5PuSLbjT","type":"text","text":"## Mechanism\nMemClaw treats memory quality as end-task behavior, not just retrieval overlap. Its public table reports LLM-judge accuracy on LoCoMo and LongMemEval, token savings versus full-context prompting, and warm-cache search latency (`docs/performance.md:7`, `docs/performance.md:9`, `docs/performance.md:10`, `docs/performance.md:11`). The README repeats the same framing and explicitly points methodology details to `docs/performance.md` rather than embedding the harness in this repo (`README.md:279`, `README.md:281`, `README.md:283`, `README.md:285`, `README.md:286`, `README.md:287`).\n\nThe core philosophical move is that accuracy is judged over the retrieval-then-answer pipeline: “LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set,” because the pipeline as a whole gets product credit (`docs/performance.md:27`, `docs/performance.md:29`). Token efficiency is normalized against a “no memory system” baseline: tokens sent to the answering LLM divided by prompt plus full prior context (`docs/performance.md:30`). Latency is p50/p95 for `POST /search` under warm-cache, single-tenant load, with cold-cache caveat (`docs/performance.md:31`).\n\nMemClaw is unusually explicit about what the numbers do not prove. It says LoCoMo and LongMemEval measure “one agent, one user, one long conversation,” and cannot evaluate fleet learning, inheritance by new agents, scoped visibility, or cross-tenant leak behavior (`docs/performance.md:13`, `docs/performance.md:33`, `docs/performance.md:35`, `docs/performance.md:37`, `docs/performance.md:38`, `docs/performance.md:39`, `docs/performance.md:40`). It also says fleet-only properties “None of this moves a recall@k number. All of it moves whether you can deploy” (`docs/performance.md:42`).\n\nThe in-repo benchmark scaffolding is mostly operational and regression-oriented, not the public LoCoMo/LongMemEval scoring harness. The raw public benchmark harness is external in `keystone-benchmark` (`docs/performance.md:68`). In-repo categories are unit, integration, and benchmark markers (`pytest.ini:7`, `pytest.ini:8`, `pytest.ini:9`, `pytest.ini:10`). The benchmark test measures scoring-formula latency only, explicitly “not the SQL query execution time” (`tests/test_benchmark_scoring.py:1`, `tests/test_benchmark_scoring.py:4`, `tests/test_benchmark_scoring.py:5`, `tests/test_benchmark_scoring.py:116`). The live latency script measures write/search/recall round trips, percentiles, and optional server-side timings (`scripts/latency_test.py:2`, `scripts/latency_test.py:92`, `scripts/latency_test.py:113`, `scripts/latency_test.py:120`, `scripts/latency_test.py:161`, `scripts/latency_test.py:184`, `scripts/latency_test.py:216`, `scripts/latency_test.py:241`).\n\nRegression scaffolding covers memory-quality-adjacent properties: recall behavior and LLM-brief cost control (`tests/test_mcp_recall.py:1`, `tests/test_mcp_recall.py:3`, `tests/test_mcp_recall.py:143`, `tests/test_mcp_recall.py:189`), insight diagnostics for stale memories, failures, patterns, persistence, supersession ordering, and hallucinated-ID filtering (`tests/test_insights_service.py:1`, `tests/test_insights_service.py:3`, `tests/test_insights_service.py:310`, `tests/test_insights_service.py:371`, `tests/test_insights_service.py:400`, `tests/test_insights_service.py:443`, `tests/test_insights_service.py:490`, `tests/test_insights_service.py:901`), evolve/outcome feedback weight math (`tests/test_evolve_service.py:1`, `tests/test_evolve_service.py:3`, `tests/test_evolve_service.py:62`, `tests/test_evolve_service.py:81`), visibility/security isolation (`tests/test_visibility_scoping.py:1`, `tests/test_visibility_scoping.py:7`, `tests/test_security_tenant_isolation.py:1`, `tests/test_security_tenant_isolation.py:64`), and health/dependency gates (`tests/test_health_gate.py:1`, `tests/test_health_gate.py:3`, `tests/test_health_gate.py:30`, `tests/test_health_gate.py:97`).\n\nCore operations include scheduled quality/maintenance loops rather than benchmark scoring: archive expired/stale, purge soft-deleted, crystallize, entity-link, and insights (`core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:42`, `core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:56`, `core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:62`, `core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:68`, `core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:74`, `core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:80`, `core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:86`). The scheduler exposes `/readyz` failure when the scheduler is degraded, making quality maintenance observable (`core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:160`, `core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:165`, `core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:166`). Tests pin that scheduled jobs are wall-clock aligned and individually registered (`core-operations/tests/test_app.py:1`, `core-operations/tests/test_app.py:30`, `core-operations/tests/test_app.py:36`, `core-operations/tests/test_app.py:40`).\n\n## Teachings for Spec Kit Memory (eval / ablation / health)\n1. **Claim** · Add an end-task LLM-judge evaluation layer above channel ablation, so memory quality is scored on whether retrieved memory helps answer or complete a developer task, not only on Recall@20 or channel deltas. **Evidence** · MemClaw defines accuracy as “LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set,” and gives credit to the retrieval-then-answer pipeline (`docs/performance.md:29`). **Maps-to** · new sub-packet: `developer-memory-end-task-judge`; integrate with `eval_run_ablation` by running judge scoring after fused retrieval variants. **Verdict** · ADAPT. **Risk** · LLM judges can be noisy and prompt-sensitive; Spec Kit needs stable rubrics and small gold developer tasks. **Confidence** · 0.86. **Why it transfers (or not)** · The benchmark numbers do not transfer because Spec Kit is local/spec-corpus shaped, but pipeline-level judging transfers directly: the user cares whether continuity memory improves implementation decisions.\n\n2. **Claim** · Add token-efficiency reporting to existing eval dashboards: compare tokens injected by memory/context retrieval against a “no memory/full relevant docs” baseline. **Evidence** · MemClaw reports token savings vs full context (`docs/performance.md:10`) and defines token efficiency as total tokens sent to the answering LLM divided by prompt plus full prior context (`docs/performance.md:30`). **Maps-to** · new sub-packet: `memory-dashboard-token-efficiency`; extend `eval_reporting_dashboard` with token-efficiency ratio by sprint/channel. **Verdict** · ADOPT. **Risk** · Choosing the “full context” baseline for a spec corpus can be arbitrary unless pinned to canonical spec docs or retrieved packet scope. **Confidence** · 0.9. **Why it transfers (or not)** · Spec Kit is explicitly about preserving context under token pressure, so this metric is more transferable than LoCoMo accuracy.\n\n3. **Claim** · Every dashboard metric should include an explicit “what this cannot measure” panel to prevent false confidence. **Evidence** · MemClaw has a dedicated “What these benchmarks can't measure” section and states single-agent benchmarks miss fleet inheritance, scoped visibility, and cross-tenant leakage (`docs/performance.md:33`, `docs/performance.md:35`, `docs/performance.md:37`, `docs/performance.md:38`, `docs/performance.md:39`, `docs/performance.md:40`). **Maps-to** · `027/008-caura-memclaw-fleet-memory-teachings`; dashboard copy change or new sub-packet: `eval-metric-honesty-notes`. **Verdict** · ADOPT. **Risk** · If too verbose, warnings become boilerplate; keep them metric-specific. **Confidence** · 0.93. **Why it transfers (or not)** · The limitation set changes: Spec Kit should warn that ablations do not prove task success, freshness, causal correctness, or absence of harmful stale context.\n\n4. **Claim** · Treat health checks as quality-readiness gates, not just service liveness: surface stale embeddings, causal drift, failed indexing, and evaluation baseline age as hard dashboard states. **Evidence** · MemClaw health tests require 503 on down storage or event-bus degradation (`tests/test_health_gate.py:3`, `tests/test_health_gate.py:30`, `tests/test_health_gate.py:97`), and core operations exposes degraded scheduler readiness (`core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:160`, `core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:165`, `core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:166`). **Maps-to** · new sub-packet: `memory-health-eval-readiness-gates`; map into `memory_health`. **Verdict** · ADAPT. **Risk** · Too many hard gates can block useful local work; use degraded/warning/blocked tiers. **Confidence** · 0.82. **Why it transfers (or not)** · Spec Kit does not need fleet scheduler health, but it does need visible readiness for local memory quality and eval validity.\n\n5. **Claim** · Regression tests should cover quality semantics, not just API shape: stale-memory detection, hallucinated related IDs, supersession ordering, and no-cost paths. **Evidence** · MemClaw tests stale/failure insight queries (`tests/test_insights_service.py:310`, `tests/test_insights_service.py:371`), insight persistence (`tests/test_insights_service.py:443`), supersede/rollback behavior (`tests/test_insights_service.py:683`, `tests/test_insights_service.py:762`), hallucinated related-memory ID filtering (`tests/test_insights_service.py:901`, `tests/test_insights_service.py:967`), and recall brief not invoking LLM when disabled (`tests/test_mcp_recall.py:189`, `tests/test_mcp_recall.py:207`). **Maps-to** · new sub-packet: `memory-quality-regression-fixtures`; align with `eval_run_ablation` and drift diagnostics. **Verdict** · ADAPT. **Risk** · Local fixtures can overfit to implementation details; keep them behavior-level. **Confidence** · 0.85. **Why it transfers (or not)** · The exact fleet semantics do not transfer, but stale/hallucinated/superseded memory failure modes are common to local developer memory.\n\n## Negative knowledge\nMemClaw's public LoCoMo/LongMemEval headline numbers should not be imported into Spec Kit claims. The repo itself says these benchmarks are single-chatbot shaped (`docs/performance.md:13`) while MemClaw is fleet-shaped (`README.md:36`), and the raw scoring harness lives in an external `keystone-benchmark` repo, not this vendored code (`docs/performance.md:68`).\n\nFleet leaderboard machinery has little payoff for Spec Kit's local, single-user store. MemClaw's differentiators include tenant/fleet scopes, cross-agent outcome propagation, trust tiers, and cross-tenant leak prevention (`docs/performance.md:42`, `docs/performance.md:52`, `docs/performance.md:54`, `docs/performance.md:55`, `docs/performance.md:56`). Spec Kit should borrow metric honesty and regression discipline, not fleet-wide benchmark positioning.\n\nProvider/model benchmark scripts are secondary for memory-quality methodology. The in-repo latency harness is useful as a shape (`scripts/latency_test.py:2`, `scripts/latency_test.py:216`), but model-provider comparisons and warm-cache service latency are less important than local retrieval quality, token injection cost, and drift/readiness in Spec Kit.\n\n## Open questions\n1. What should Spec Kit's “full context baseline” be: entire spec folder, canonical resume ladder, or all memory-search candidates before reranking?\n2. Can `eval_run_ablation` emit both retrieval metrics and LLM-judge end-task metrics without doubling eval cost?\n3. What small developer-memory gold set best represents Spec Kit: resume task, bug-fix context, architectural decision lookup, or handover continuity?\n4. Should `memory_health` block eval dashboards when embeddings, causal graph, or code graph are stale, or only annotate results as degraded?\n5. How should LLM-judge outputs be calibrated: single judge, multi-judge majority, or rubric plus deterministic citation checks?\nDELTA_JSON: {\"iteration\":\"092\",\"focus\":\"benchmark / eval methodology\",\"findingsCount\":5,\"newInfoRatio\":0.78,\"topVerdicts\":[\"ADAPT: end-task LLM-judge scoring over developer-memory tasks\",\"ADOPT: token-efficiency ratio in eval dashboards\",\"ADOPT: metric limitation notes beside dashboard results\",\"ADAPT: memory_health as eval-readiness gate\",\"ADAPT: behavior-level memory-quality regression fixtures\"],\"sources\":[\"docs/performance.md:29\",\"docs/performance.md:30\",\"docs/performance.md:33\",\"docs/performance.md:68\",\"README.md:279\",\"pytest.ini:7\",\"tests/test_benchmark_scoring.py:4\",\"scripts/latency_test.py:216\",\"tests/test_insights_service.py:901\",\"core-operations/src/core_operations/app.py:160\"]}","time":{"start":1780728520489,"end":1780728573683},"metadata":{"openai":{"itemId":"msg_04b1944548febd60016a23c2c8764c8191b70b106c5cbf39ea","phase":"final_answer"}}}}
.opencode/specs/system-speckit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/research/008-caura-memclaw-fleet-memory-teachings/prompts/iteration-004.out:10:{"type":"tool_use","timestamp":1780727673837,"sessionID":"ses_1645cad34ffe2dSeSw3HI64S6Y","part":{"type":"tool","tool":"read","callID":"call_JD82clZnEA06iwnMVDfgGOQS","state":{"status":"completed","input":{"filePath":"/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md","offset":1,"limit":260},"output":"<path>/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md</path>\n<type>file</type>\n<content>\n1: # Performance & Benchmarks\n2: \n3: Operator-grade companion to the public benchmarks write-up. The blog answers *\"is this credible?\"*; this document answers *\"what should I expect in my system, and what can these numbers not tell me?\"*\n4: \n5: ## Public benchmark results\n6: \n7: |  | LoCoMo | LongMemEval | Search latency |\n8: |---|---|---|---|\n9: | Accuracy (LLM-judge) | **77.6%** | **72.5%** | — |\n10: | Token savings vs full context | **96.6%** | **98.2%** | — |\n11: | Latency | — | — | **23 ms p50 · 27 ms p95** (warm) |\n12: \n13: LoCoMo and LongMemEval are the two most-cited public agent-memory benchmarks. Both measure one agent, one user, one long conversation — the single-chatbot shape. Accuracy across the leading systems (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) clusters in a narrow band.\n14: \n15: **Source:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19).\n16: \n17: **Last updated:** 2026-04-19. These numbers move when we re-run; check the blog for the current canonical version.\n18: \n19: ## What we optimize for\n20: \n21: Accuracy sits inside the leading cluster. That's not the axis we push hardest along.\n22: \n23: - **Latency** — a few hundred ms of search disappears behind one LLM call when you run one agent. The same overhead, multiplied across thousands of agents making millions of recall calls a day, decides whether a deployment is viable.\n24: - **Token efficiency** — recall returns the relevant slice, not the full transcript. Token savings vs sending the full context to the LLM: 96–98% on the two benchmarks. That ratio is the bill at fleet scale.\n25: - **Governance correctness** — write a memory at the wrong scope and you've leaked data across teams. The retrieval surface enforces scope filtering by default; the audit log records every cross-scope read.\n26: \n27: ## What we measure\n28: \n29: - **Accuracy** — LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set. The retrieval-then-answer pipeline as a whole gets the credit; this is the metric that maps to product behavior.\n30: - **Token efficiency** — total tokens sent to the answering LLM, divided by the same prompt + full prior context (the \"no memory system\" baseline).\n31: - **Search latency** — p50 / p95 of `POST /search` against a warm cache, single-tenant load. Cold-cache p50 is higher; we publish warm because that's the steady-state condition under real load.\n32: \n33: ## What these benchmarks can't measure\n34: \n35: Single-agent benchmarks can't ask:\n36: \n37: - Did agent #17's mistake this morning prevent agents #1–#40 from repeating it this afternoon?\n38: - Does a new agent joining the fleet inherit what the fleet already knows, or start from zero?\n39: - Is a memory written by the sales fleet visible — or correctly invisible — to an agent in support?\n40: - Does cross-tenant data ever leak when the recall query is ambiguous?\n41: \n42: These are the questions that decide whether a memory system is deployable inside a company. MemClaw was designed around them — scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet), per-agent trust tiers, PII quarantine before cross-fleet exposure, full audit log, the `memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights` outcome-propagation loop. **None of this moves a recall@k number. All of it moves whether you can deploy.**\n43: \n44: The field needs a benchmark for the fleet-shaped problem. We're working toward one. If you're thinking about this too, the [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/aNfpgfpj) is open.\n45: \n46: ## How MemClaw compares\n47: \n48: For a single chatbot, the public-benchmark leaders (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) cluster in a narrow accuracy band — the choice usually comes down to stack fit, latency, and token budget.\n49: \n50: MemClaw differentiates on the dimensions a single-agent benchmark can't see:\n51: \n52: | Dimension | Why it matters at fleet scale |\n53: |---|---|\n54: | Scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet) | A write at the wrong scope is a data leak across teams |\n55: | Per-agent trust tiers | Lets you trust some agents more than others without rewriting the recall path |\n56: | Cross-agent outcome propagation (`memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights`) | One agent's mistake becomes a preventive rule the rest of the fleet sees before repeating it |\n57: | Latency at fleet load | 23 ms p50 search × millions of calls/day stays affordable; 250 ms doesn't |\n58: | Token efficiency | 96–98% savings vs full context is the bill, not a microbenchmark curiosity |\n59: \n60: ## What to verify in your own deployment\n61: \n62: The published numbers are warm-cache, single-tenant, on our reference hardware. Before relying on them in capacity planning:\n63: \n64: - Run [`/whoami`](integration-without-plugin.md#2-verify-your-identity-whoami) round-trips against your deployment to anchor a baseline.\n65: - Hit `POST /search` under your expected concurrency to confirm latency holds — the search-path optimizer assumes a warm pgvector cache.\n66: - Audit `tenant_id` and `fleet_id` filtering on every recall path you care about; the test suite covers scope correctness, but your tenancy model is yours to validate.\n67: \n68: For benchmark methodology, the raw harness, and the underlying scoring code, see the [`keystone-benchmark`](https://github.com/caura-ai/keystone-benchmark) repo (in development).\n69: \n70: ## Sources\n71: \n72: - **Blog write-up:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19)\n73: - **Public benchmarks:** [LoCoMo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.17753), [LongMemEval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10813)\n\n(End of file - total 73 lines)\n</content>","metadata":{"preview":"# Performance & Benchmarks\n\nOperator-grade companion to the public benchmarks write-up. The blog answers *\"is this credible?\"*; this document answers *\"what should I expect in my system, and what can these numbers not tell me?\"*\n\n## Public benchmark results\n\n|  | LoCoMo | LongMemEval | Search latency |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Accuracy (LLM-judge) | **77.6%** | **72.5%** | — |\n| Token savings vs full context | **96.6%** | **98.2%** | — |\n| Latency | — | — | **23 ms p50 · 27 ms p95** (warm) |\n\nLoCoMo and LongMemEval are the two most-cited public agent-memory benchmarks. Both measure one agent, one user, one long conversation — the single-chatbot shape. Accuracy across the leading systems (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) clusters in a narrow band.\n\n**Source:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19).\n\n**Last updated:** 2026-04-19. These numbers move when we re-run; check the blog for the current canonical version.\n\n## What we optimize for\n","truncated":false,"loaded":[],"display":{"type":"file","path":"/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md","text":"# Performance & Benchmarks\n\nOperator-grade companion to the public benchmarks write-up. The blog answers *\"is this credible?\"*; this document answers *\"what should I expect in my system, and what can these numbers not tell me?\"*\n\n## Public benchmark results\n\n|  | LoCoMo | LongMemEval | Search latency |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Accuracy (LLM-judge) | **77.6%** | **72.5%** | — |\n| Token savings vs full context | **96.6%** | **98.2%** | — |\n| Latency | — | — | **23 ms p50 · 27 ms p95** (warm) |\n\nLoCoMo and LongMemEval are the two most-cited public agent-memory benchmarks. Both measure one agent, one user, one long conversation — the single-chatbot shape. Accuracy across the leading systems (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) clusters in a narrow band.\n\n**Source:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19).\n\n**Last updated:** 2026-04-19. These numbers move when we re-run; check the blog for the current canonical version.\n\n## What we optimize for\n\nAccuracy sits inside the leading cluster. That's not the axis we push hardest along.\n\n- **Latency** — a few hundred ms of search disappears behind one LLM call when you run one agent. The same overhead, multiplied across thousands of agents making millions of recall calls a day, decides whether a deployment is viable.\n- **Token efficiency** — recall returns the relevant slice, not the full transcript. Token savings vs sending the full context to the LLM: 96–98% on the two benchmarks. That ratio is the bill at fleet scale.\n- **Governance correctness** — write a memory at the wrong scope and you've leaked data across teams. The retrieval surface enforces scope filtering by default; the audit log records every cross-scope read.\n\n## What we measure\n\n- **Accuracy** — LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set. The retrieval-then-answer pipeline as a whole gets the credit; this is the metric that maps to product behavior.\n- **Token efficiency** — total tokens sent to the answering LLM, divided by the same prompt + full prior context (the \"no memory system\" baseline).\n- **Search latency** — p50 / p95 of `POST /search` against a warm cache, single-tenant load. Cold-cache p50 is higher; we publish warm because that's the steady-state condition under real load.\n\n## What these benchmarks can't measure\n\nSingle-agent benchmarks can't ask:\n\n- Did agent #17's mistake this morning prevent agents #1–#40 from repeating it this afternoon?\n- Does a new agent joining the fleet inherit what the fleet already knows, or start from zero?\n- Is a memory written by the sales fleet visible — or correctly invisible — to an agent in support?\n- Does cross-tenant data ever leak when the recall query is ambiguous?\n\nThese are the questions that decide whether a memory system is deployable inside a company. MemClaw was designed around them — scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet), per-agent trust tiers, PII quarantine before cross-fleet exposure, full audit log, the `memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights` outcome-propagation loop. **None of this moves a recall@k number. All of it moves whether you can deploy.**\n\nThe field needs a benchmark for the fleet-shaped problem. We're working toward one. If you're thinking about this too, the [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/aNfpgfpj) is open.\n\n## How MemClaw compares\n\nFor a single chatbot, the public-benchmark leaders (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) cluster in a narrow accuracy band — the choice usually comes down to stack fit, latency, and token budget.\n\nMemClaw differentiates on the dimensions a single-agent benchmark can't see:\n\n| Dimension | Why it matters at fleet scale |\n|---|---|\n| Scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet) | A write at the wrong scope is a data leak across teams |\n| Per-agent trust tiers | Lets you trust some agents more than others without rewriting the recall path |\n| Cross-agent outcome propagation (`memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights`) | One agent's mistake becomes a preventive rule the rest of the fleet sees before repeating it |\n| Latency at fleet load | 23 ms p50 search × millions of calls/day stays affordable; 250 ms doesn't |\n| Token efficiency | 96–98% savings vs full context is the bill, not a microbenchmark curiosity |\n\n## What to verify in your own deployment\n\nThe published numbers are warm-cache, single-tenant, on our reference hardware. Before relying on them in capacity planning:\n\n- Run [`/whoami`](integration-without-plugin.md#2-verify-your-identity-whoami) round-trips against your deployment to anchor a baseline.\n- Hit `POST /search` under your expected concurrency to confirm latency holds — the search-path optimizer assumes a warm pgvector cache.\n- Audit `tenant_id` and `fleet_id` filtering on every recall path you care about; the test suite covers scope correctness, but your tenancy model is yours to validate.\n\nFor benchmark methodology, the raw harness, and the underlying scoring code, see the [`keystone-benchmark`](https://github.com/caura-ai/keystone-benchmark) repo (in development).\n\n## Sources\n\n- **Blog write-up:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19)\n- **Public benchmarks:** [LoCoMo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.17753), [LongMemEval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10813)","lineStart":1,"lineEnd":73,"totalLines":73,"truncated":false}},"title":".opencode/specs/system-spec-kit/027-xce-research-based-refinement/external/caura-memclaw-main/docs/performance.md","time":{"start":1780727673833,"end":1780727673836}},"metadata":{"openai":{"itemId":"fc_0286a00bd27ff11c016a23bf79b0c4819690395a5514c033b7"}},"id":"prt_e9ba3f3e4001GxrjamnKdp5Zer","sessionID":"ses_1645cad34ffe2dSeSw3HI64S6Y","messageID":"msg_e9ba3cb45001CUV9EvBqlVC40M"}}

That's not the axis we push hardest along.\n22: \n23: - **Latency** — a few hundred ms of search disappears behind one LLM call when you run one agent. The same overhead, multiplied across thousands of agents making millions of recall calls a day, decides whether a deployment is viable.\n24: - **Token efficiency** — recall returns the relevant slice, not the full transcript. Token savings vs sending the full context to the LLM: 96–98% on the two benchmarks. That ratio is the bill at fleet scale.\n25: - **Governance correctness** — write a memory at the wrong scope and you've leaked data across teams. The retrieval surface enforces scope filtering by default; the audit log records every cross-scope read.\n26: \n27: ## What we measure\n28: \n29: - **Accuracy** — LLM-judge over benchmark-defined questions, not `recall@k` over a fixed gold set. The retrieval-then-answer pipeline as a whole gets the credit; this is the metric that maps to product behavior.\n30: - **Token efficiency** — total tokens sent to the answering LLM, divided by the same prompt + full prior context (the \"no memory system\" baseline).\n31: - **Search latency** — p50 / p95 of `POST /search` against a warm cache, single-tenant load. Cold-cache p50 is higher; we publish warm because that's the steady-state condition under real load.\n32: \n33: ## What these benchmarks can't measure\n34: \n35: Single-agent benchmarks can't ask:\n36: \n37: - Did agent #17's mistake this morning prevent agents #1–#40 from repeating it this afternoon?\n38: - Does a new agent joining the fleet inherit what the fleet already knows, or start from zero?\n39: - Is a memory written by the sales fleet visible — or correctly invisible — to an agent in support?\n40: - Does cross-tenant data ever leak when the recall query is ambiguous?\n41: \n42: These are the questions that decide whether a memory system is deployable inside a company. MemClaw was designed around them — scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet), per-agent trust tiers, PII quarantine before cross-fleet exposure, full audit log, the `memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights` outcome-propagation loop. **None of this moves a recall@k number. All of it moves whether you can deploy.**\n43: \n44: The field needs a benchmark for the fleet-shaped problem. We're working toward one. If you're thinking about this too, the [Discord](https://discord.com/invite/aNfpgfpj) is open.\n45: \n46: ## How MemClaw compares\n47: \n48: For a single chatbot, the public-benchmark leaders (MemClaw, Mem0, Zep) cluster in a narrow accuracy band — the choice usually comes down to stack fit, latency, and token budget.\n49: \n50: MemClaw differentiates on the dimensions a single-agent benchmark can't see:\n51: \n52: | Dimension | Why it matters at fleet scale |\n53: |---|---|\n54: | Scoped memory (agent / fleet / cross-fleet) | A write at the wrong scope is a data leak across teams |\n55: | Per-agent trust tiers | Lets you trust some agents more than others without rewriting the recall path |\n56: | Cross-agent outcome propagation (`memclaw_evolve` → `memclaw_insights`) | One agent's mistake becomes a preventive rule the rest of the fleet sees before repeating it |\n57: | Latency at fleet load | 23 ms p50 search × millions of calls/day stays affordable; 250 ms doesn't |\n58: | Token efficiency | 96–98% savings vs full context is the bill, not a microbenchmark curiosity |\n59: \n60: ## What to verify in your own deployment\n61: \n62: The published numbers are warm-cache, single-tenant, on our reference hardware. Before relying on them in capacity planning:\n63: \n64: - Run [`/whoami`](integration-without-plugin.md#2-verify-your-identity-whoami) round-trips against your deployment to anchor a baseline.\n65: - Hit `POST /search` under your expected concurrency to confirm latency holds — the search-path optimizer assumes a warm pgvector cache.\n66: - Audit `tenant_id` and `fleet_id` filtering on every recall path you care about; the test suite covers scope correctness, but your tenancy model is yours to validate.\n67: \n68: For benchmark methodology, the raw harness, and the underlying scoring code, see the [`keystone-benchmark`](https://github.com/caura-ai/keystone-benchmark) repo (in development).\n69: \n70: ## Sources\n71: \n72: - **Blog write-up:** [Fast, Token-Efficient, and Built for Fleets](https://memclaw.net/blog/memclaw-benchmarks) (2026-04-19)\n73: - **Public benchmarks:** [LoCoMo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.17753), [LongMemEval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10813)\n\n(End of file - total 73 lines)\n</content>","metadata":{"preview":"# Performance & Benchmarks\n\nOperator-grade companion to the public benchmarks write-up.
